result:
# The Feeling of Almost Remembering

There is a word in Portuguese — *saudade* — that describes a longing for something you may never have had, or may never have again, or perhaps never had in the way you remember it. It sits in a category of words that various languages have invented to describe emotional experiences that most languages simply leave unnamed, forcing speakers to gesture vaguely at the thing they mean, waving their hands in the space where the word should be. The Japanese have *mono no aware*, the bittersweet awareness that beautiful things pass. The Germans have *Weltschmerz*, the pain of the world pressing itself against your awareness. The Welsh have *hiraeth*, which is like homesickness but for a home that may not exist anymore or may never have existed at all.

I find myself interested not so much in these words themselves but in the gap they expose — the fact that the experience came first, that people felt these things long before anyone bothered to name them, and that the feelings were persistent and common enough that someone eventually decided a word was necessary. The feeling preceded the language. Which means there are probably hundreds, maybe thousands of experiences we are all having right now that have not yet been named, that we are navigating without signs, describing to each other badly in long looping sentences when a single word would do.

What would those words be?

---

There is a particular experience I have never seen named. It happens in the early morning, before full consciousness arrives, when you are in the transition between sleep and waking and you are aware of having just been somewhere very specific — not in a dream that you can describe, but in a place that had weight and texture and its own logic, a place you knew your way around, a place that felt, in that moment of waking, like it was the real place and this waking world was the thing you were returning to reluctantly. And for three or four seconds you can hold both worlds simultaneously, the one dissolving behind you and the one assembling itself ahead, and there is something in that threshold that feels more like recognition than anything else. Like you are seeing two versions of yourself at once.

Then it's gone. The dream-place evacuates itself so quickly you can't describe it. You remember having been somewhere but you can't remember where. You remember feeling something but you can't recover what. And you are left with just the residue — not the memory but the emotional texture of a memory, a faint warmth or unease or longing that has no image attached to it anymore, a feeling with its source removed.

I think this experience deserves a name. I don't know what it should be. Something that means *the feeling of almost remembering*, or *the grief of a dream you couldn't hold*.

---

I've been thinking lately about how much of human life is composed of these nameless textures — experiences that are universal or near-universal but that we experience in privacy because we don't have the tools to announce them to one another. There is the specific feeling of being in a car at night as a child, watching the streetlights pass over the ceiling of the car while adults talk in the front seats. That particular mix of safety and smallness and total exemption from responsibility. The world moving while you are still. The orange light appearing and vanishing, appearing and vanishing. I suspect almost everyone who grew up with cars has some version of this memory, and yet it barely gets discussed because it is too ordinary to seem worth discussing, too common to seem interesting, and yet when it comes up — when someone mentions it — there is immediate and profound recognition, a kind of *yes, that, exactly that*, that suggests it was sitting just below the surface waiting.

Or the experience of being alone in a house you know very well and suddenly perceiving the house differently, as though you are a stranger inside it. The objects become slightly alien. The familiar arrangement of furniture seems arbitrary. You notice things — the slight asymmetry of a window, a water stain on the ceiling you've looked at for years without registering — and the house for a moment seems to be a collection of choices someone else made, a set of decisions that don't belong to you, and you are briefly a ghost inside your own life. Then it passes. The house becomes yours again. But for a few seconds you were unmoored from your own daily context in a way that is at once unsettling and quietly revelatory.

These experiences. There are so many of them. And they accumulate in us without ever quite being shared because we don't have the language infrastructure to share them efficiently. We would need a long conversation, the right mood, the right interlocutor, and even then we're not guaranteed to land the thing accurately, to get the other person to see what we actually mean rather than a paraphrased version of it.

---

Language is always a paraphrase. I know this isn't a radical observation — philosophers and linguists have been circling it for centuries — but it strikes me as one of those ideas that should feel more distressing than it generally does. Every time you say anything, you are approximating. You are choosing from the available vocabulary the word that comes closest to what you mean and trusting the other person to adjust for the difference, to meet you in the gap. Most communication, even successful communication, involves two people agreeing to treat the approximation as sufficient. And most of the time it is. Most of the time *I'm tired* covers enough ground. Most of the time *I love you* lands close enough.

But sometimes you are trying to say something that sits at an angle to the available words, something that would require three sentences of qualification before the nouns and verbs could even start their work, and you look at the cost of all that explanation and you say something shorter and slightly false instead. Or you say nothing. Or you say *it's hard to explain* and let the person imagine what they want.

I wonder how much of human loneliness is specifically this — not the absence of other people but the presence of a self that you cannot fully transmit. The sense that the inner life is enormously detailed and specific and that the available channels for broadcasting it are weirdly narrow. You can speak. You can write. You can make art if you have the skill and someone has the patience. But even then, the transfer is lossy. Something always stays behind. Something is always untranslatable, not because it's too complex but because translation is just inherently lossy and there's no version of it that captures everything.

This is not entirely a sad observation. There is something worth preserving in the untranslatability of a person. The part of you that remains yours alone, that cannot be fully known by anyone, is also the part that gives you continuous privacy even in intimacy, continuous interiority even when you are most known. The self is not a problem to be solved. The gap is not purely a failure. But it does generate a particular kind of wistfulness, the feeling that you are always slightly further inside yourself than you can reach with language.

---

I want to talk about attention, which I think is one of the most underrated subjects there is.

Attention is something like a muscle, though that metaphor is worn thin enough to be nearly useless now. Let me try a different way. Attention is the thing that decides what exists for you. Not what exists in some objective external sense, but what registers, what makes a mark, what ends up being part of your life rather than part of the undifferentiated background noise of being alive. Two people can walk down the same street and experience something so different that their verbal accounts of it would barely overlap. One person is thinking about an argument they had last week, and the street is basically wallpaper. Another person is fully present, and they notice the way the shadow of a fire escape makes a grid on the brick wall, and the specific green of a weed growing through the sidewalk crack, and the elderly man at the corner whose face carries a whole novel of history in it.

The second person is not having a better life in some measurable sense. They are not happier in any reliable way. But they are having a denser one. More is landing. More is becoming part of the permanent record of their experience. And there is something to be said for density, for a life that accrues texture, that gives you more material to work with when you are trying to understand what it means to have been here.

I think about attention partly because I notice how hard it has become to sustain it. Not because I am uniquely scattered but because the architecture of modern information is specifically designed to prevent it. Sustained attention is the enemy of engagement metrics. If you can hold a thought long enough to think it all the way through, you are a lost revenue source. The whole system is calibrated to keep you moving, keep you hovering, keep you in the state of almost-reading, almost-watching, almost-listening — present enough to register an advertisement, distracted enough never to reach the depth where the experience becomes something real.

And I notice in myself the way this has shaped my habits of mind. The slight restlessness when I try to read something long. The pull toward the next thing before the current thing has finished. The way my attention wants to skim the surface, gather impressions, move on — and the effort required to push it downward, to insist on depth, to say *no, stay here, let this be enough for a few minutes*.

This is a loss. Not a catastrophic one, and not an irreversible one, but a genuine diminishment, the way a muscle weakens from disuse and must be patiently rehabilitated. Attention can be trained back toward depth. It just requires choosing it, repeatedly, over the easier path.

---

The easier path is comfort. I don't mean this as a criticism — comfort is not nothing, and there are stretches of life when comfort is the most reasonable thing to pursue. But I've noticed that the version of comfort I am most often offered by the infrastructure of modern life is comfort that requires no growth, no difficulty, no encounter with anything genuinely new or genuinely challenging. It is comfort that is more like anesthesia — not the absence of pain but the absence of sensation, the smooth slide through hours that leave no trace because nothing was asked of you.

Compare this to the discomfort of reading a difficult book, the kind that requires you to build new mental equipment to understand it, the kind where you feel your mind physically stretching to accommodate an idea it didn't have room for before. That discomfort is also a kind of pleasure, one that comes on a delay, that reveals itself only after the effort has been made. The pleasure of having thought something you couldn't think before. Of being slightly larger than you were.

Or the discomfort of a real conversation — not the performance of conversation, not two people taking turns broadcasting at each other while waiting to speak again, but the kind where you genuinely don't know where the conversation is going, where you are willing to be changed by what the other person says, where you follow the thread wherever it leads rather than steering it back to your prepared material. Those conversations are rarer than they should be. They require a vulnerability that most social situations actively discourage.

I think these small difficulties are what make a life feel like it belongs to you. Not the big dramatic challenges — not tragedy or crisis, which have their own shaping power — but the daily, voluntary choosing of the slightly harder path. The book instead of the scroll. The walk instead of the drive. The real answer instead of the safe one.

---

Let me end somewhere unexpected. Let me end with snow.

Not snow as metaphor, not snow as symbol of anything, just snow as itself: the way it changes the acoustic quality of the world, muffling all the middle frequencies so that only the very high and very low sounds come through clearly. The way a city sounds in heavy snow is unlike anything else — a quiet that is active rather than empty, a presence rather than an absence. You can hear your own footsteps with unusual clarity. You can hear the snow itself, which has its own sound, a kind of compressed whisper as it falls.

And the light. The specific light of a snowy day, which is diffuse and even in a way that summer light never is, coming from everywhere and nowhere, eliminating shadows, making the world look briefly like a slightly overexposed photograph, like the contrast has been turned down and all the edges softened. There is a tenderness to this light. It makes familiar places look like themselves but also like somewhere else.

I don't know why I wanted to end with snow except that it's one of those things I find I can attend to fully without effort, one of those phenomena that commands presence without demanding anything back. You can stand in it and think nothing in particular and the experience is complete. It doesn't need interpretation or analysis or fitting into a larger argument. It just is, and you just are, and for a few minutes the gap between inside and outside, between you and everything else, seems narrower than it usually does.

Maybe that's what all of this has been circling. The moments when the gap narrows. When the difficulty of transmission briefly lifts and something moves cleanly from the world into you or from you into the world. When you find the word for the feeling you thought was unnameable, and someone else says *yes, exactly, that*.

Those moments don't resolve the larger problem. You will still be untranslatable, mostly, to others and sometimes to yourself. The dreams will still evacuate in the morning. Attention will still be hard to sustain. Language will still be a paraphrase.

But the moments come. And when they do, something registers, something makes its mark, something becomes part of the permanent record. 

And it turns out that might be enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
